skill.name: senior-security
skill.description: Security engineering workflow skill with local Python scaffolds for threat modeling, security audits, pentest planning, and reference guidance.
skill.summary: Run local security workflow scaffolds and review security reference guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many high-risk patterns, but review found they are documentation snippets, metadata text, or local scaffold scripts. No prompt injection, network access, credential exfiltration, shell execution, or cryptographic implementation code was found. The only residual concern is local script execution with optional user-selected JSON output paths.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled Local Report Output
low_findings.description: The scripts write JSON results to the path supplied with --output. This is normal report behavior, but it can overwrite files writable by the current user.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and confirmed in all three scripts. Risk is low because output requires an explicit CLI option and no network or privilege escalation exists.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Command Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis labeled fenced markdown and shell examples as Ruby backtick execution. The locations are documentation commands, not executable code paths inside the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown code fences or usage examples. No Ruby files or shell backtick execution statements were found at these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Indicators
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged security and cryptography wording as weak algorithm usage. The referenced files contain generic documentation text and no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or crypto calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found placeholder reference prose rather than cryptographic implementation. No evidence found for weak algorithms or insecure crypto code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Environment Setup Example
low_findings.description: The .env finding is a setup command that copies an example file to a local .env file. It does not read, print, transmit, or harvest environment secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is visible in a documentation setup block. No code reads .env values, accesses process environments, or sends secrets elsewhere.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Filesystem Write
dangerous_patterns.description: Three Python scripts can write JSON reports to a user-provided output path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operation is confirmed, but it is an explicit local reporting feature with no hidden destination.

content.value_statement: Security work needs repeatable review steps across architecture, code, and test planning. This skill provides local scaffolds and references for threat modeling, audits, and pentest workflow design.
actual_capabilities: Validates a target project path before running local workflow scaffolds.
Runs placeholder threat modeling, security audit, and pentest planning scripts.
Prints a concise status report for each script run.
Can write script results as formatted JSON when requested.
Provides reference pages for architecture, penetration testing, and cryptography topics.
limitations: The scripts are scaffolds and do not perform deep vulnerability analysis.
No live scanning, exploitation, or external system testing is built in.
The reference files contain generic guidance, not complete security standards.
Output files can overwrite paths writable by the current user.

use_cases.title: Standardize review setup
use_cases.description: Create a repeatable starting point for local threat modeling and security audit workflows.

use_cases.title: Prepare secure design reviews
use_cases.description: Use the references to frame architecture questions before implementation or release.

use_cases.title: Draft pentest plans
use_cases.description: Use the pentest scaffold to organize authorized testing plans and reporting steps.

prompt_templates.title: Run a basic audit scaffold
prompt_templates.prompt: Use senior-security to run the security auditor scaffold on [PROJECT_PATH]. Summarize the report and note any missing setup.

prompt_templates.title: Create a threat model outline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the threat modeling scaffold for [PROJECT_PATH]. Build a threat model outline for assets, trust boundaries, threats, and mitigations.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an authorized pentest
prompt_templates.prompt: Use senior-security to draft an authorized pentest workflow for [APPLICATION]. Include scope, prerequisites, phases, and reporting needs.

prompt_templates.title: Review architecture and crypto choices
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the security architecture and cryptography references to review [DESIGN]. Identify assumptions, risky decisions, and safer alternatives.

output_examples.input: Run a local security audit scaffold for ./service.
output: The report shows success status and zero generated findings.
Next step: add project-specific checks to the analysis method.

output_examples.input: Prepare a threat model outline for a new API.
output: Assets include user records, tokens, audit logs, and service credentials.
Trust boundaries include client traffic, API gateway, service network, and data stores.
Primary risks include broken authorization, injection, replay, and secret exposure.
Recommended mitigations include validation, least privilege, logging, and key rotation.

output_examples.input: Draft a pentest planning summary for a web app.
output: Define written scope and authorization before testing.
Map application entry points and authentication flows.
Prioritize manual validation for high-impact findings.
Produce a final report with evidence, severity, and remediation guidance.

best_practices: Run scripts only against projects and systems you are authorized to assess.
Review generated output before using it in security decisions.
Customize scaffold analysis logic for your technology stack and risk model.
anti_patterns: Treating scaffold output as a complete vulnerability assessment.
Running pentest workflows against third-party systems without written approval.
Writing reports to sensitive paths or shared directories without review.

faq.question: Does this skill perform real vulnerability scanning?
faq.answer: No. The included scripts are scaffolds. They need custom checks before they can perform meaningful vulnerability analysis.

faq.question: Does it send project data to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed scripts use local paths, console output, and optional local JSON files only.

faq.question: Can I use it for penetration testing?
faq.answer: Use it for authorized planning and workflow structure. It does not include exploit execution or live network scanning.

faq.question: What tools does it support?
faq.answer: The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What should I customize first?
faq.answer: Start by adding project-specific checks to each script analysis method and defining report fields your team needs.

faq.question: Why is the security risk low?
faq.answer: Static high-risk alerts were false positives. The remaining risk is local script execution and user-selected report file output.
